title: Inhaled Respiratory Medications
subtitle: The nurse teaches inhaler use — a fast-acting rescue inhaler for sudden symptoms, a daily maintenance inhaler for prevention, using a spacer, and rinsing the mouth after a steroid inhaler.

patient.name: Inhalers
patient.admitDate: The nurse teaches safe and correct use of inhaled medications.

labs.v: Steroid inhaler is not a rescue.

nursesNote: The nurse teaches correct use of inhaled respiratory medications. A short-acting bronchodilator (rescue inhaler) such as albuterol works quickly to relieve sudden symptoms like wheezing or shortness of breath and is used as needed. A maintenance (controller) inhaler, such as an inhaled corticosteroid or a long-acting bronchodilator, is taken daily to prevent attacks and does not relieve an acute attack, so it is never used as a rescue. When both a bronchodilator and a steroid inhaler are scheduled together, the bronchodilator is used first to open the airways, then the steroid, and the patient waits about a minute between puffs of the same inhaler. A spacer used with a metered-dose inhaler improves delivery to the lungs and reduces medication left in the mouth. Good technique includes a slow, deep breath coordinated with the puff and holding the breath for about ten seconds. After using a steroid inhaler, the patient rinses the mouth and spits to prevent oral thrush and hoarseness. Mild tremor and a faster heart rate are expected effects of a short-acting bronchodilator. Needing the rescue inhaler more often than usual signals poor control and should be reported to the provider.
providerOrders: Teach the rescue inhaler for sudden symptoms and the maintenance inhaler for daily prevention
Use the bronchodilator before the steroid inhaler when scheduled together
Teach correct technique and use of a spacer
Instruct the patient to rinse the mouth after a steroid inhaler
Tell the patient to report increasing rescue inhaler use

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about inhaled medications?

opts.text: A short-acting bronchodilator relieves sudden symptoms
A maintenance inhaler is taken daily to prevent attacks
A steroid inhaler is the rescue for an acute attack
Rinse the mouth after a steroid inhaler
A spacer improves delivery to the lungs
Use the bronchodilator before the steroid
Wait about a minute between puffs of the same inhaler
Take a slow, deep breath and hold for ~10 seconds
Mild tremor with albuterol is an emergency
Needing rescue inhaler more often means poor control
Daily controller use does not stop an acute attack
Rinsing after a steroid inhaler prevents thrush

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order using a scheduled bronchodilator and steroid inhaler

opts.text: Shake the inhaler and attach the spacer
Use the bronchodilator first
Wait about a minute, then use the steroid inhaler
Rinse the mouth after the steroid
Evaluate breathing and document
Sudden wheezing and shortness of breath
A scheduled daily dose to prevent attacks
An inhaled steroid taken every morning
Albuterol before exercise that triggers symptoms
A long-acting bronchodilator twice daily
Quick relief during an acute asthma attack

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A patient with asthma is acutely wheezing and breathless
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Sit the patient upright
Give the short-acting rescue inhaler
Reassess breathing and oxygen saturation
Notify the provider if no improvement
Document the event and response
Using the bronchodilator before the steroid inhaler
Using a steroid inhaler to stop a sudden attack
Rinsing the mouth after the steroid inhaler
Firing all puffs in one quick breath
Using a spacer with the metered-dose inhaler
Stopping the daily controller once feeling well
Reporting rising rescue inhaler use
Exhaling hard into the inhaler before a puff
